questions.question: Explain the Delegation Event Model in Java Swing with reference to the Event Source, Event Listener, and Event Object.
questions.hint: Remember: Event source delegates the responsibility of handling events to external listener objects
keyPoints: Event Source: The component that generates events (e.g., JButton, JTextField)
Event Listener: The object that receives and processes events
Event Object: Encapsulates event-related information (type, source, timestamp)
Delegation: Event handling is delegated to separate listener objects rather than the source itself
Register listener using addXXXListener() methods
Loose coupling between source and handler

questions.question: What is the Model-View-Controller (MVC) design pattern in Swing? Explain its components.
questions.hint: Swing simplifies MVC to Model-Delegate where the UI object acts as both View and Controller
keyPoints: Model: Contains data and business logic (state of the component)
View: Visual representation of the component (how it looks)
Controller: Handles user interactions and updates model/view
In Swing: MVC is implemented as Model-Delegate
Separation of concerns: Allows independent development of each layer
Examples: JTable has TableModel, TableColumn, TableCellRenderer

questions.question: List and explain any four common event listener interfaces in Swing.
questions.hint: Each listener interface has specific methods that must be implemented
keyPoints: ActionListener: Handles action events (button clicks, menu selections)
MouseListener: Handles mouse button press, release, click, enter, exit
KeyListener: Handles key press, release, and type events
WindowListener: Handles window opened, closed, activated, etc.
ItemListener: Handles item selection events (checkboxes, radio buttons)
FocusListener: Handles focus gained and lost events

questions.question: Explain two key features that distinguish Swing from AWT.
questions.hint: Think about component hierarchy and runtime customization
keyPoints: Lightweight vs Heavyweight: Swing components are lightweight, AWT are heavyweight
Pluggable Look and Feel: Swing allows changing appearance at runtime
MVC Architecture: Swing components follow MVC pattern
Additional Components: Swing provides more sophisticated components (JTree, JTable)
Platform Independence: Swing uses peer-less lightweight components

questions.question: What is the purpose of UIManager in Swing? Explain with an example.
questions.hint: UIManager is used to set the visual style of Swing components
keyPoints: UIManager manages look and feel resources and defaults
setLookAndFeel() method changes the visual appearance
getSystemLookAndFeelClassName() gets system default
Available Look and Feels: Metal, Nimbus, System, Motif
Cross-platform look is provided by default (Metal/Nimbus)
Example: UIManager.setLookAndFeel(UIManager.getSystemLookAndFeelClassName())

questions.question: Explain the Delegation Event Model in detail with a neat diagram showing the event flow. Also write a program demonstrating event handling for a JButton click.
questions.hint: Draw the flow: User Action → Event Source → Event Object → Event Listener → Handle Event
keyPoints: Diagram showing: Event Source → Event Object → Event Listener
Event Source generates events (JButton, JTextField)
Event Object carries event information (ActionEvent, MouseEvent)
Event Listener implements specific interface (ActionListener)
Registration using addActionListener()
Program: Import javax.swing.*, java.awt.*
Program: Create JFrame, JButton, implement ActionListener
Program: Override actionPerformed() method
Program: Register listener, set visible

questions.question: Describe the MVC architecture as implemented in Swing components. Explain with reference to JTable component showing how Model, View, and Controller work together.
questions.hint: JTable demonstrates MVC clearly with separate model for data
keyPoints: Model: Stores data (TableModel interface - DefaultTableModel)
View: Displays data (JTable view component)
Controller: Handles user interaction (selection, editing)
JTable Example: TableModel stores row/column data
JTable Example: JTable renders the visual representation
JTable Example: Cell editors handle user input
Separation allows changing data without changing UI
AbstractTableModel and DefaultTableModel classes
Fire table data change methods notify views of updates

questions.question: Explain the event handling mechanism in Swing. Discuss the different types of events and the corresponding listener interfaces with their methods.
questions.hint: Group events by their source and understand the listener-method mapping
keyPoints: Event-driven programming model
Event classes hierarchy: AWTEvent → ActionEvent, MouseEvent, KeyEvent, etc.
Listener interfaces require implementing all methods
Adapter classes provide default empty implementations
ActionEvent + ActionListener (actionPerformed)
MouseEvent + MouseListener (mouseClicked, mousePressed, mouseReleased, mouseEntered, mouseExited)
KeyEvent + KeyListener (keyPressed, keyReleased, keyTyped)
WindowEvent + WindowListener (windowOpened, windowClosing, etc.)
Adapter classes reduce boilerplate code

questions.question: Compare and contrast Lightweight and Heavyweight components in Java Swing. Also explain why mixing them can cause issues.
questions.hint: Think about platform dependence and the component peer system
keyPoints: Heavyweight: Use native peers, depend on underlying OS
Lightweight: Draw their own appearance, no native peers
Heavyweight examples: java.awt.Button, TextField
Lightweight examples: javax.swing.JButton, JTextField
Swing components are primarily lightweight
Mixing issues: Z-order conflicts, painting problems
Heavyweight components always appear on top of lightweight
Solution: Use all Swing components or set content pane opaque
Lightweight advantages: Pluggable Look and Feel, faster, portable

questions.question: Design a Swing application demonstrating the use of multiple event listeners. Your application should have at least three different types of UI components and handle events from each of them.
questions.hint: Use separate listener classes or implement multiple interfaces
keyPoints: Create JFrame with setDefaultCloseOperation
Add JButton for ActionEvent handling
Add JCheckBox for ItemEvent handling
Add JTextField for FocusEvent handling
Implement ActionListener for button click
Implement ItemListener for checkbox state change
Implement FocusListener for text field focus
Use addXXXListener() methods to register
Override appropriate methods for each listener
Display output using System.out.println or JOptionPane

questions.question: Write a complete Java Swing program to create a login form with username and password fields. Implement ActionListener to validate credentials when the Login button is clicked.
questions.hint: Use getPassword() method for JPasswordField (returns char array)
keyPoints: Import javax.swing.*, java.awt.*, java.awt.event.*
Create class implementing ActionListener
Create JFrame with GridLayout (rows=3, cols=2)
Add JLabel for 'Username:' and JTextField
Add JLabel for 'Password:' and JPasswordField
Add JButton for 'Login'
Override actionPerformed() method
Get text from JTextField and JPasswordField
Validate: if username='admin' and password='admin', show success
Otherwise show error using JOptionPane
Register button with addActionListener(this)

questions.question: Write a program to create a Swing application that demonstrates the Pluggable Look and Feel feature. Include code to switch between at least two different look and feels.
questions.hint: Set Look and Feel before creating any Swing components
keyPoints: Import javax.swing.*, javax.swing.UIManager.*
Use try-catch for ClassNotFoundException, InstantiationException
UIManager.setLookAndFeel() method
Option 1: UIManager.getSystemLookAndFeelClassName()
Option 2: 'javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel'
Option 3: 'javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel'
Option 4: 'com.sun.java.swing.plaf.windows.WindowsLookAndFeel'
Create and display JFrame after setting Look and Feel
Add components (JButton, JCheckBox, JTextField)
Call SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI() for dynamic update

questions.question: Create a Swing application with a JTextArea that counts and displays the number of characters as the user types. Use appropriate event listeners.
questions.hint: Consider using DocumentListener instead of KeyListener for better text change detection
keyPoints: Import javax.swing.*, java.awt.*, java.awt.event.*
Create JFrame and JTextArea with rows and columns
Create JLabel to display character count
Implement KeyListener interface
Override keyTyped() method to capture character input
Get text length using textArea.getText().length()
Update JLabel with character count
Register KeyListener to JTextArea
Alternative: Use DocumentListener for better handling
Handle edge cases like backspace

questions.question: Write a program to create a simple calculator GUI using Swing. Implement ActionListener to handle button clicks and perform basic arithmetic operations.
questions.hint: Use a state variable to track calculator operations
keyPoints: Create JFrame with GridLayout (4x4)
Create JTextField for display (non-editable)
Create buttons for digits 0-9
Create buttons for operators (+, -, *, /)
Create buttons for Clear (C) and Equals (=)
Implement ActionListener interface
Store first operand, operator, and second operand
In actionPerformed(): handle digit buttons append to display
Handle operator buttons: store first operand and operator
Handle equals: calculate result based on operator
Handle clear: reset all variables and display
Use try-catch for NumberFormatException and ArithmeticException
